response: Decision recorded above (keep cron, reject Conductor and the wrapper), with the two concrete revisit triggers, and saved to memory so future sessions on this repo know not to re-litigate it unless one of those triggers actually fires.

options.label: Keep cron script
options.summary: Continue running nightly_sync.py via cron as-is; revisit adoption if job count or failure rate crosses a stated threshold.

options.label: Adopt Conductor now
options.summary: Stand up the Conductor orchestration framework (scheduler, metadata DB, workers) and migrate the single sync job onto it.

options.label: Lightweight retry-and-alert wrapper
options.summary: Add a small wrapper providing retries and alerting around the existing script, without adopting a full framework.

O1.crux: Zero failures across 14 months of a single 41-line job leaves no reliability gap for any tooling to close.
O1.dependency: If job count or failure rate is already trending toward the stated revisit-threshold (not just hypothetically someday), the status quo stops being the safe default.

O2.crux: Standing up a scheduler, metadata DB, and worker pool creates a permanent operational burden for a two-person team to run exactly one job that has never failed.
O2.dependency: If growth to many interdependent jobs is concrete and near-term rather than speculative, paying the fixed cost now (before a painful mid-flight migration) could flip this.

O3.crux: Retries and alerting are cheap insurance against the one failure mode cron truly can't handle (silent failure), without taking on framework operational overhead.
O3.dependency: If cron plus existing monitoring already surfaces failures reliably (i.e., there's no alerting gap), the wrapper adds maintenance surface for a risk that isn't actually uncovered.

O1.crux: One 41-line job with a 30-day 100% success streak and no operational surface has no problem for a framework to solve.
O1.dependency: If the job count or complexity grows (or single-script cron becomes unable to retry/alert on failures), the calculus reverses toward wrapper or framework.

O2.crux: Conductor's standing operational cost (scheduler, metadata DB, workers, upgrade treadmill) is permanent while the problems it solves (DAGs, lineage, backfills) don't exist for a single reliable job.
O2.dependency: If the team is about to scale to a dozen+ interdependent jobs imminently (not hypothetically), the upfront investment could be justified now rather than later.

O3.crux: A small retry/alert wrapper closes the one real gap (cron can't retry or page) without taking on a framework's operational surface.
O3.dependency: If cron's current failure mode is already adequately visible/handled (e.g., existing monitoring catches failures fine), the wrapper adds complexity for a problem that's already covered.

O1.crux: Zero failures across 14 months and 30 logged runs, single job, near-zero operational surface — no problem exists for Conductor to solve.
O1.dependency: If the job's failure rate or job count is actually about to change (e.g. team already planning several new coupled jobs), the flat-cost/no-benefit calculus reverses.

O2.crux: Standing up a scheduler, metadata DB, and worker pool plus a version-upgrade treadmill for one 41-line job that has never paged anyone is a large fixed operational cost against near-zero present benefit.
O2.dependency: If the team is confidently about to scale to a dozen+ interdependent jobs very soon, front-loading the framework cost now (vs. migrating later) could be the right call.

O3.crux: Retries/backoff and alerting are the only Conductor features that map to a real (if currently unrealized) risk — silent failure — and a thin wrapper buys that without a standing service to operate.
O3.dependency: If cron plus existing monitoring already pages on failure (i.e. alerting risk is already covered), the wrapper adds cost for no new coverage and O1 becomes strictly better.

O1.dependency: If job count or failure rate is already trending toward the stated revisit-threshold (not just hypothetically someday), the status quo stops being the safe default.

O2.crux: Standing up a scheduler, metadata DB, and worker pool creates a permanent operational burden for a two-person team to run exactly one job that has never failed.
O2.dependency: If growth to many interdependent jobs is concrete and near-term rather than speculative, paying the fixed cost now (before a painful mid-flight migration) could flip this.
